stem: A 34-year-old G1P1 woman attends her GP at 8 weeks postpartum with a 4-week history of low mood, anhedonia, marked early-morning waking despite the infant sleeping through, ruminative guilt about her mothering and a recently-completed EPDS score of 17 with positive endorsement of item 10 (passive death ideation, no plan, no intent, no access). Risk assessment with the GP confirms no active suicidal plan, no psychotic features and no infanticidal thoughts. She has previously had an episode of moderate depression at age 24 that responded well to sertraline, which she continued for 12 months without significant adverse effects, then ceased without recurrence. She is breastfeeding her healthy term infant exclusively, the infant is thriving on the 50th centile, and she wishes to continue breastfeeding. She has no other medications, no medical comorbidities, no QT-prolonging drugs and a normal ECG taken antenatally. She is open to medication alongside psychological therapy and is already on a perinatal CBT waitlist with a likely 4-week wait. She has no family history of bipolar disorder, no postpartum psychosis history and her partner is supportive at home. She is keen to start treatment quickly.
lead_in: Which antidepressant is the most appropriate first-line choice?

options.text: Sertraline 50 mg orally daily, titrating to clinical response over 2-4 weeks
options.rationale: Sertraline has the strongest breastfeeding safety data with very low milk:plasma ratio and infant relative-dose typically below 2%, and she has a prior excellent response to it. RANZCP and LactMed list it as a preferred SSRI in breastfeeding (RANZCP Perinatal MH; LactMed).

options.text: Fluoxetine 20 mg orally daily as a first-line breastfeeding-compatible SSRI
options.rationale: Fluoxetine and its long-half-life active metabolite norfluoxetine accumulate in the breastfed infant and have caused colic, irritability and poor feeding in case reports; it is not preferred when starting de novo (LactMed; RANZCP).

options.text: Citalopram 20 mg orally daily as the initial choice while she is breastfeeding
options.rationale: Citalopram has higher milk transfer and more adverse infant reports than sertraline and is associated with dose-dependent QT prolongation; it is an acceptable second-line but not first-line when starting fresh (LactMed; AMH).

options.text: Mirtazapine 30 mg orally at night for sleep and appetite while breastfeeding
options.rationale: Mirtazapine is reasonable when sedation/appetite are dominant features but data in lactation are more limited than sertraline; not first-line for a typical perinatal MDE presentation (RANZCP).

options.text: Defer all pharmacotherapy and await perinatal CBT in approximately 4 weeks
options.rationale: Moderate-to-severe symptoms with EPDS 17 and passive ideation warrant combined psychological plus pharmacological therapy; deferring medication risks deterioration during the wait (RANZCP; beyondblue Perinatal MH guidelines).

stem: A 32-year-old G2P2 woman, 5 days post-vaginal birth of a healthy term infant, presents to the emergency department with a 6-hour history of pleuritic right-sided chest pain, mild dyspnoea on exertion and a swollen tender right calf. Bedside observations and ECG are detailed below. CTPA confirms a segmental right lower lobe pulmonary embolus and Doppler ultrasound shows acute right popliteal-femoral DVT. She has no haemodynamic instability, no right heart strain and no hypoxia at rest. She is exclusively breastfeeding her term, well infant and is highly motivated to continue. She has no other medical conditions, takes no medications, has no allergies, no history of bleeding disorder and her postnatal Hb is 118 g/L with normal platelets, INR and APTT. She has no recent surgery other than the vaginal birth and no contraindication to anticoagulation. The hospital pharmacist and team need to choose an anticoagulant compatible with breastfeeding for the initial and ongoing treatment period. The team specifically requires an agent with established safety in breastfeeding and a known reversal pathway in the puerperium, given the increased early postpartum bleeding risk.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate anticoagulant regimen?

options.text: Therapeutic enoxaparin transitioning to warfarin with bridging until INR therapeutic; continue breastfeeding
options.rationale: Both LMWH (negligible oral bioavailability in the infant) and warfarin (high protein binding, undetectable in milk) are compatible with breastfeeding and are the RANZCOG-recommended options for postpartum VTE; DOACs are NOT recommended in lactation due to insufficient safety data (RANZCOG VTE; LactMed).

options.text: Apixaban 10 mg twice daily for 7 days then 5 mg twice daily, continuing breastfeeding
options.rationale: DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran) are not recommended in lactation; manufacturer data and LactMed advise against use because safety in the breastfed infant is not established (LactMed; AMH).

options.text: Rivaroxaban 15 mg twice daily for 21 days then 20 mg daily, continuing breastfeeding
options.rationale: Rivaroxaban is excreted into breast milk in animal studies and is not recommended in lactation; LMWH or warfarin are preferred (LactMed; AMH).

options.text: Unfractionated heparin infusion only with discontinuation of breastfeeding for the treatment period
options.rationale: UFH is also compatible with breastfeeding but is not preferred over LMWH for routine VTE outside of renal failure or imminent procedure; cessation of breastfeeding is unnecessary (RANZCOG).

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily and graduated compression stockings as primary treatment for VTE
options.rationale: Aspirin is not adequate treatment for an acute PE or DVT; therapeutic anticoagulation is required (RANZCOG; eTG).

stem: A 71-year-old G4P4 postmenopausal woman attends a urogynaecology outpatient clinic with 18 months of progressive vaginal pressure that is now associated with a visible bulge at the introitus when she stands at the end of the day, occasional difficulty completing defaecation that improves when she splints the posterior vaginal wall, and intermittent urinary hesitancy. She has had no postmenopausal bleeding, no smear abnormality (HPV negative 2 years ago) and no dyspareunia (not currently sexually active). She has well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine, BMI 28, never-smoker, chronic constipation managed with osmotic laxatives. Speculum and POP-Q examination on maximal Valsalva in the supine position record the following measurements: Aa -1, Ba +1, C +3, GH 5 cm, PB 3 cm, TVL 9 cm, Ap -2, Bp -2, D +1. The most dependent point of prolapse is therefore the cervix at +3 cm beyond the hymen. On bivalve examination her vaginal walls show appropriate support of the anterior and posterior compartments with no significant cystocele or rectocele beyond the hymen. PVR is 30 mL. She wishes to discuss the diagnosis before considering treatment options.
lead_in: Which is the most accurate description of this prolapse?

options.text: POP-Q stage 2 anterior compartment prolapse (cystocele)
options.rationale: The most dependent point is C (cervix) at +3, not Ba; the anterior wall is only at Ba +1, which is stage 2 in isolation, but the dominant defect is apical (RANZCOG; IUGA).

options.text: POP-Q stage 3 apical (uterine) prolapse
options.rationale: POP-Q staging uses the most dependent point: C at +3 (>1 cm beyond hymen but TVL is 9 cm so not complete eversion) is stage 3, with the apex (uterus) being the dominant compartment. Stage 4 would require complete eversion (most distal point at +/- TVL - 2 cm) (IUGA/ICS).

options.text: POP-Q stage 4 complete vaginal vault eversion
options.rationale: Stage 4 requires the most distal point at TVL minus 2 cm or beyond (essentially complete eversion); cervix at +3 with TVL 9 cm does not meet this criterion, and vault prolapse implies prior hysterectomy (IUGA).

options.text: POP-Q stage 3 posterior compartment prolapse (rectocele)
options.rationale: Bp is -2, well above the hymen; the posterior compartment is not the dominant or staged defect (IUGA).

options.text: POP-Q stage 1 mixed compartment prolapse
options.rationale: Stage 1 requires the most distal point >1 cm above the hymen; cervix at +3 is well beyond that threshold (IUGA).

stem: A 64-year-old G3P3 postmenopausal woman attends urogynaecology with 12 months of progressive vaginal vault prolapse 9 years after a total abdominal hysterectomy for benign menorrhagia. She has previously trialled a ring pessary for 6 months with recurrent expulsion despite refit, and supervised pelvic floor physiotherapy for 4 months with no symptomatic benefit. POP-Q examination shows vaginal vault descent to +4 with anterior wall to +2 and posterior wall to 0; TVL is 8 cm. She is sexually active and wishes to preserve coital function; she has no obesity-related surgical risk (BMI 24), no significant cardiopulmonary disease, no diabetes and is otherwise healthy with no contraindication to general anaesthetic and abdominal surgery. She has been counselled on the TGA-restricted status of transvaginal mesh kits and the residual indications for synthetic mesh. She and her partner have made an informed decision to proceed with definitive surgical management and have specifically asked about the highest-durability option that preserves vaginal length and sexual function. She has been counselled that the highest-durability vault prolapse repair preserving vaginal length and coital function in a fit woman is laparoscopic or robotic sacrocolpopexy with type 1 polypropylene mesh fixed to the anterior longitudinal ligament of the sacrum.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate surgical procedure?

options.text: Laparoscopic sacrocolpopexy with type 1 polypropylene mesh fixed to the anterior sacral ligament
options.rationale: Sacrocolpopexy (open or laparoscopic) using type 1 macroporous polypropylene mesh is the gold-standard procedure for symptomatic vault prolapse where coital function is to be preserved; it has the lowest recurrence rate and best preserves vaginal length; abdominal (non-vaginal) mesh placement is not subject to the TGA's transvaginal mesh restriction (RANZCOG; NICE NG123).

options.text: Transvaginal mesh kit repair of the vaginal vault using a synthetic mesh device
options.rationale: Transvaginal mesh kits for prolapse repair were withdrawn by the TGA in Australia in 2017 due to harm signals and are not available (TGA mesh statement).

options.text: Sacrospinous ligament fixation of the vaginal vault through a vaginal surgical approach
options.rationale: Sacrospinous fixation is a reasonable native-tissue alternative, particularly for patients who are poor candidates for abdominal surgery, but has higher recurrence and tends to shorten and posteriorly deviate the vagina; sacrocolpopexy is more durable and preserves vaginal axis in fit candidates (RANZCOG; NICE).

options.text: Le Fort colpocleisis with obliteration of the vagina for definitive vault support
options.rationale: Colpocleisis is an obliterative procedure reserved for older, frail, non-sexually-active women who accept loss of coital function; she remains sexually active (RANZCOG).

options.text: Repeat pessary trial with a Gellhorn pessary plus indefinite topical oestrogen therapy
options.rationale: She has already failed conservative management with recurrent pessary expulsion and ineffective PFMT and now elects surgery; another pessary is unlikely to succeed for a stage 3 vault defect with this profile (RANZCOG).

stem: A 52-year-old G3P3 woman attends with 12 months of urinary leakage. On a 3-day bladder diary she records: 8 leakage episodes total - 6 preceded by sudden urgency (mostly on the way to the toilet or hearing running water), and 2 small-volume leaks on coughing or sneezing. Daytime void frequency is 9, nocturia 2, urgency score on Patient Perception of Bladder Condition is severe, and quality-of-life impact is dominated by the urge episodes (she has stopped attending her grandson's school events because of fear of leakage with running to a toilet). PVR is 40 mL, urinalysis is negative, and a cough stress test reproduces a small leak with full bladder. BMI 31, no smoking, no relevant medications, no neurological symptoms. She declines surgery at this stage and has not yet had any structured conservative therapy or pharmacotherapy. She is willing to commit to a 3-month structured conservative trial before considering any pharmacological or surgical option. There are no red flags such as visible haematuria, recurrent UTI or palpable mass and her cervical screening is up to date. She is open to commencing a structured 3-month conservative trial now.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line management approach?

options.text: PFMT, bladder training, weight loss and caffeine reduction; review at 3 months
options.rationale: NICE and Continence Foundation Australia advise structured conservative therapy as initial management for mixed UI, with treatment then directed at the predominant symptom (urge here). PFMT helps both components, bladder training targets urge, and weight loss helps both; structured trial for at least 3 months before escalation (NICE NG123; Continence Foundation Australia).

options.text: Refer immediately for a tension-free vaginal tape mid-urethral sling procedure
options.rationale: Surgery is not first-line for mixed UI, and stress-incontinence surgery may worsen pre-existing urge symptoms; she also declines surgery (RANZCOG; NICE NG123).

options.text: Commence an antimuscarinic plus duloxetine combination from the outset of management
options.rationale: Combined pharmacotherapy from outset is not recommended; conservative therapy is first-line, with single-agent pharmacotherapy directed at the predominant symptom if conservative therapy is inadequate (NICE).

options.text: Refer for invasive urodynamic studies before offering any conservative management
options.rationale: Urodynamics are not required before conservative management of uncomplicated mixed UI; they are reserved for diagnostic uncertainty, prior incontinence surgery, suspected voiding dysfunction or where surgery is being planned (NICE NG123).

options.text: Insert a continence pessary and commence indefinite topical oestrogen therapy
options.rationale: Continence pessaries are useful adjuncts mainly for stress UI driven by hypermobility, and topical oestrogen is reserved for urogenital atrophy; neither addresses her predominant urge component (Continence Foundation Australia).

stem: A 72-year-old G3P3 postmenopausal woman with type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.4%, on metformin + dapagliflozin) presents with 6 months of small-volume continuous urinary leakage, daytime urinary frequency 12, nocturia 4 with leakage on rising, and a sensation of incomplete emptying. She has no urgency that precedes the leakage. Her GP has been treating her empirically as urge incontinence with oxybutynin for 8 weeks with worsening of her leakage. On examination she has a palpable bladder to the umbilicus, mildly impaired ankle reflexes with reduced light touch and pinprick over both feet bilaterally, and no costovertebral angle tenderness. Speculum reveals a stage 2 cystocele to the hymen with no other prolapse. Urinalysis: protein 1+, no nitrites, no leucocytes. Bedside bladder scan post-void shows a PVR of 720 mL. Bloods: Hb 118 g/L, creatinine 142 micromol/L (baseline 88), eGFR 36 mL/min/1.73m2 (down from 68), K+ 5.3 mmol/L, HbA1c 8.4%. Renal tract ultrasound shows bilateral hydronephrosis. She is on no other medications that obviously precipitate retention and is not currently constipated. She lives independently and has supportive family. The team is being asked to differentiate chronic high-pressure urinary retention with post-obstructive AKI from primary urge or stress incontinence, particularly given the clinical picture of bladder distension to the umbilicus, very high PVR, new renal impairment and bilateral hydronephrosis on imaging. The unit has 24-hour urology cover and capacity for immediate catheterisation and assessment for safe relief of retention.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Cease oxybutynin, insert indwelling catheter and refer urgently to urology
options.rationale: PVR 720 mL with new bilateral hydronephrosis and rising creatinine indicates high-pressure chronic retention with obstructive AKI - bladder decompression with an indwelling catheter and urgent urology review is the immediate priority; antimuscarinics worsen retention and must be stopped (NICE NG112; Continence Foundation Australia; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Add mirabegron 50 mg daily to the oxybutynin and review again in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Adding further pharmacotherapy for assumed OAB ignores high-volume retention with renal compromise; doing so risks worsening retention and renal injury (NICE NG123).

options.text: Refer for urgent mid-urethral sling surgery for presumed stress urinary incontinence now
options.rationale: She has overflow incontinence from chronic retention, not stress incontinence; a sling in this context will precipitate complete retention and worsen renal injury (NICE).

options.text: Commence frusemide 40 mg daily to manage her presumed nocturnal polyuria
options.rationale: Frusemide increases urine production into an already overloaded obstructed bladder and worsens both retention and AKI (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Arrange outpatient urodynamics in 6 weeks and continue current therapy unchanged
options.rationale: Outpatient delay is unsafe when there is post-obstructive AKI with bilateral hydronephrosis; immediate bladder decompression is required (NICE; Continence Foundation Australia).

stem: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman attends a vulval clinic with 10 months of vulval and vaginal soreness, burning dyspareunia and recurrent contact-bleeding from the vagina. On examination there is glazed erythema of the labia minora and vestibule with a serpiginous white reticular lacy pattern at the inner labia, painful erosions at the introitus, and on speculum examination there is friable erythematous mucosa of the upper vagina with synechiae causing partial vaginal narrowing and obliteration of the vaginal fornices. There is no labial fusion, no figure-of-eight pallor extending around the anus, and the clitoral hood is intact and not buried. She also has lacy white striae on the buccal mucosa and a desquamative gingivitis confirmed by her dentist 6 months ago. There are no skin lesions on the trunk or limbs. She has no urinary symptoms, BMI 24, on no medications, never smoked. Vulval biopsy is taken from the edge of an erosion and shows a band-like sub-epithelial lymphocytic infiltrate with basal cell vacuolar degeneration and Civatte bodies; there is no homogeneous dermal hyalinisation. She has no skin lesions on the trunk or limbs and no genital herpes history.
lead_in: Which diagnosis is most consistent with these findings?

options.text: Erosive vulvovaginal lichen planus with classic mucocutaneous involvement
options.rationale: Wickham striae, painful erosions, vaginal involvement with synechiae and friability, concurrent oral lichen planus and the histology of band-like lymphocytic infiltrate with Civatte bodies all point to erosive lichen planus; vaginal involvement is a key feature that distinguishes LP from LS (BSSVD; RCH-Vulval Disorders guidance via Royal Women's Vic).

options.text: Lichen sclerosus with figure-of-eight pallor and architectural change
options.rationale: LS shows porcelain-white atrophic skin with figure-of-eight perianal involvement, labial resorption and clitoral hood phimosis; vaginal mucosa is NOT involved in LS, and histology shows dermal hyalinisation, which is absent here (BSSVD).

options.text: Vulvar intraepithelial neoplasia (HSIL/uVIN) on the vulval mucosa
options.rationale: HSIL/uVIN shows discrete pigmented or whitish plaques with acetowhitening and HPV-related dysplastic epithelium on histology; the inflammatory band, Civatte bodies and oral involvement here are not VIN features (RANZCOG; BSSVD).

options.text: Plasma cell vulvitis (Zoon vulvitis) of the vulval vestibule
options.rationale: Zoon vulvitis presents with glistening orange-red patches without Wickham striae or oral lesions, and histology shows a dense plasma cell infiltrate, not band-like T-cell infiltrate with Civatte bodies (BSSVD).

options.text: Atrophic vaginitis from genitourinary syndrome of menopause
options.rationale: GSM produces diffuse pallor, loss of rugae and dryness without erosions, Wickham striae or oral involvement; histology is not erosive lymphocytic (RANZCOG: Menopause).

stem: A 31-year-old G3P2 Aboriginal woman from a remote community, with known severe rheumatic mitral stenosis (MVA 1.0 cm2, mean gradient 14 mmHg, PASP 56 mmHg on third-trimester echo, NYHA II antenatally) presents to a regional referral centre at 39+2 weeks in spontaneous labour. She has been on monthly BPG, metoprolol 50 mg BD, and prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg daily, ceased 18 hours ago. She is 6 cm dilated, contracting 4-in-10, the foetus is cephalic with reassuring CTG, and an epidural has been sited and is providing good analgesia. Over the last 30 minutes she has become increasingly breathless, oxygen saturation has fallen from 98% to 91% on room air, HR has risen from 96 to 132 (sinus tachycardia on ECG with no shortened diastole arrhythmia and no fibrillatory waves), BP is 105/68, JVP is elevated, and she has bibasal crackles. Chest x-ray shows pulmonary venous congestion with no focal consolidation. Bedside echo confirms unchanged severe MS with no left atrial thrombus and preserved LV function. The cardiac-obstetric-anaesthetic team is gathering at the bedside, blood is cross-matched, and a high-dependency bed is available. Foetal heart rate remains 145 with normal variability and accelerations, and the obstetric assessment is that vaginal birth with assisted second stage remains feasible if she stabilises within minutes She has been counselled antenatally about the haemodynamic risks of tachycardia in severe mitral stenosis and the rationale for assisted second stage to avoid prolonged Valsalva.
lead_in: What is the single best next intervention to stabilise her acutely?

options.text: IV furosemide plus additional IV beta-blocker to lower heart rate and reduce filling pressures
options.rationale: In severe MS the haemodynamic enemy is tachycardia (shortened diastole, raised LA pressure, pulmonary oedema). Lowering rate with beta-blockade and offloading with a loop diuretic is the cornerstone of acute management while planning an assisted second stage to limit Valsalva effort. Emergency caesarean and balloon valvuloplasty are only required if she fails to stabilise (RANZCOG; ESC Pregnancy Cardiovascular Disease Guideline).

options.text: Tocolyse with IV salbutamol to halt labour and proceed to emergency percutaneous mitral balloon valvuloplasty
options.rationale: Beta-agonist tocolysis causes tachycardia and pulmonary oedema in severe MS and is contraindicated; valvuloplasty is considered antenatally in refractory disease, not in established labour at term (RHDAustralia; ESC 2018).

options.text: Proceed to immediate category 1 caesarean section under general anaesthesia for acute pulmonary oedema
options.rationale: Foetus is reassuring and labour is progressing; GA carries high haemodynamic risk in severe MS (induction-related tachycardia, swings in afterload). Vaginal birth with assisted second stage under regional anaesthesia is preferred if feasible (RANZCOG W-WH 22; ESC 2018).

options.text: Give an IV crystalloid bolus of 500 mL Hartmann's to maintain preload before considering further intervention
options.rationale: Fluid bolus in pulmonary congestion from severe MS worsens pulmonary oedema; preload must be preserved but not increased (RHDAustralia; ESC 2018).

options.text: Commence IV amiodarone infusion for presumed atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
options.rationale: ECG shows sinus tachycardia, not AF; amiodarone is reserved for arrhythmia and carries foetal thyroid risk (RHDAustralia; AMH).

stem: A 30-year-old G3P2 woman presents to ED 10 days after a medical TOP at 8 weeks using mifepristone-misoprostol from her GP. The procedure was reported as successful with passage of products on day 1 and tapering bleeding for 6 days. Over the last 36 hours she has had heavy bleeding soaking 1 large pad per hour, lower abdominal cramping, low-grade fever (37.8 deg C), foul-smelling discharge, and feels lightheaded on standing. She has no significant past history, takes no medications, blood group O Rh positive, and breastfed her two prior children. On arrival: BP 96/58, HR 116, T 37.9, RR 18, SpO2 99%. Abdomen tender suprapubically with guarding but no peritonism. Speculum: open external os with brisk bleeding and brown malodorous discharge, products visible at the os. Bedside USS: heterogeneous endometrial collection 38 mm with vascularity on colour Doppler and no free fluid in the pouch of Douglas. Bloods: Hb 88 g/L, WCC 16.4 x 10^9/L, CRP 142 mg/L, lactate 1.6, group-and-hold sent. She has had no ectopic risk factors and the pre-TOP scan confirmed intrauterine pregnancy with normal adnexa.
lead_in: What is the single best next step in management?

options.text: Outpatient repeat misoprostol 800 microg vaginal plus doxycycline 100 mg BD for 14 days, review in 48 hours
options.rationale: She has haemodynamic compromise, signs of sepsis and active bleeding; outpatient management is unsafe and inadequate source control (RANZCOG TOP; eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Resuscitate with IV access, fluids, group-and-hold, and broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, then proceed to urgent surgical evacuation of retained products
options.rationale: Septic incomplete abortion with haemorrhage requires resuscitation, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (ampicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole, or piperacillin-tazobactam per eTG), and source control by urgent surgical evacuation. Delaying evacuation prolongs the source of infection and bleeding (RANZCOG TOP; eTG Sepsis; RANZCOG PPH/Gynae emergencies).

options.text: Admit for IV oxytocin infusion with close observation, deferring evacuation pending blood culture results
options.rationale: Oxytocin has limited effect on a non-puerperal uterus, antibiotics should not wait for culture results in sepsis (within 1 hour per ANZICS), and source control must not be delayed (eTG Sepsis; ANZICS Sepsis).

options.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV with observation and serial Hb without antibiotic cover until culture results
options.rationale: TXA is adjunctive but does not address source; delaying antibiotics in sepsis breaches the 1-hour standard and risks septic shock (eTG Sepsis; ANZICS Sepsis).

options.text: Intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m2 for presumed ongoing intrauterine pregnancy and discharge home to GP
options.rationale: Methotrexate addresses neither retained products nor sepsis, and is contraindicated in active infection (AMH; eTG).

stem: A 32-year-old G3P2 woman at 30 weeks gestation discloses intimate partner violence during a private antenatal visit after the midwife has seen her alone. Her partner has been verbally and physically abusive for 3 years; the frequency and severity have escalated over the past 6 weeks since she told him she was pregnant. Last week he choked her with both hands until she briefly lost consciousness, threatened to kill her if she left, and removed her mobile phone so she could not contact her sister. He has access to firearms because he is a licensed recreational shooter. She has two children aged 4 and 6 at home, no immediate family support locally, and no current Apprehended Domestic Violence Order. She has not previously disclosed to a clinician. She is asking what to do; she is frightened, oriented, and engaged. There is a social worker on site this afternoon and a state-funded specialist DV service with crisis accommodation available. The midwife has documented the disclosure in a separate confidential section of the record that the partner cannot access The midwife now needs to triage the risk level before activating crisis response.
lead_in: Which combination of features in this disclosure most strongly indicates EXTREME risk of intimate partner femicide and mandates immediate crisis-response activation?

options.text: Frequency of arguments and verbal abuse over the past 3 years without any physical injury
options.rationale: Verbal abuse and chronic frequency are markers of abuse but are not the discriminating extreme-risk features; the specific factors below are more strongly associated with lethality (1800RESPECT; ANROWS).

options.text: A past episode of pushing without injury approximately 18 months ago and now resolved
options.rationale: Historical low-severity violence is concerning but not in the same risk class as the cluster in option C (ANROWS).

options.text: Recent non-fatal strangulation, threat to kill, escalation in pregnancy, and firearm access
options.rationale: Non-fatal strangulation, an explicit threat to kill, escalation during pregnancy, abuser access to firearms, and coercive control (such as phone removal) are the strongest single predictors of intimate partner femicide. Together they meet criteria for EXTREME risk on the Danger Assessment and DVSAT and mandate immediate crisis-response activation (NSW Health DV Routine Screening; 1800RESPECT; ANROWS; Danger Assessment).

options.text: Disclosure occurring during the antenatal visit itself, without any other contextual features
options.rationale: The act of disclosure is positive engagement, not a risk marker; risk is determined by the content of the disclosure (ANROWS).

options.text: Absence of any Apprehended Domestic Violence Order despite the long abusive relationship history
options.rationale: Lack of AVO is common; it does not by itself stratify extreme risk (1800RESPECT).
